name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّمَا جَزَٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحَارِبُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَسَادًا أَن يُقَتَّلُوٓا۟ أَوْ يُصَلَّبُوٓا۟ أَوْ تُقَطَّعَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَرْجُلُهُم مِّنْ خِلَـٰفٍ أَوْ يُنفَوْا۟ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لَهُمْ خِزْىٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (٣٣)
```

```english
Indeed the requital of those who wage war against Allah and His Apostle, and try to cause corruption on the earth, is that they shall be slain or crucified, or shall have their hands and feet cut off from opposite sides, or be banished from the land. That is a disgrace for them in this world, and in the Hereafter, there is a great punishment for them, (33)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَقْدِرُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣٤)
```

```english
excepting those who repent before you capture them, and know that Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (34)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱبْتَغُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ٱلْوَسِيلَةَ وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٣٥)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and seek the means of recourse to Him, and wage jihad in His way, so that you may be felicitous. (35)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّ لَهُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا وَمِثْلَهُۥ مَعَهُۥ لِيَفْتَدُوا۟ بِهِۦ مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَا تُقُبِّلَ مِنْهُمْ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٣٦)
```

```english
Indeed if the faithless possessed all that is on the earth, and as much of it besides, to redeem themselves with it from the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, it shall not be accepted from them, and there is a painful punishment for them. (36)
```

```arabic
يُرِيدُونَ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ وَمَا هُم بِخَـٰرِجِينَ مِنْهَا ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ (٣٧)
```

```english
They would long to leave the Fire, but they shall never leave it, and there is a lasting punishment for them. (37)
```

```arabic
وَٱلسَّارِقُ وَٱلسَّارِقَةُ فَٱقْطَعُوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَهُمَا جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَسَبَا نَكَـٰلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٣٨)
```

```english
As for the thief, man or woman, cut off their hands as a requital for what they have earned. [That is] an exemplary punishment from Allah, and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (38)
```

```arabic
فَمَن تَابَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ظُلْمِهِۦ وَأَصْلَحَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣٩)
```

```english
But whoever repents after his wrongdoing, and reforms, then Allah shall accept his repentance. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (39)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ يُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٤٠)
```

```english
Do you not know that to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth? He punishes whomever He wishes, and forgives whomever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things. (40)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses are not devoid of connection to what preceded them. Although the previous account of the son of Adam killing his brother and what Allah, the Glorified, ordained for the Children of Israel because of it was a continuation of the discourse concerning the Children of Israel and an exposition of their state—without explicitly containing a legal limit (حد) or a ruling (حكم) by direct correspondence—it is nonetheless not without a connection, by virtue of its implied content, to these verses that address the legal limit for those who spread corruption in the land and for thieves.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّما جَزاءُ الَّذِينَ يُحارِبُونَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَساداً** — "Indeed the reenactment for those who wage war against Allah and His Apostle, and strive to cause corruption on the earth..." (5:33).

"Corruption (فساداً)" is a verbal noun (مصدر) used in place of a circumstantial qualifier (حال). Although "waging war against Allah" (محاربة الله) is impossible in its literal sense, necessitating a metaphorical meaning, it possesses a broad sense that could apply to the violation of every legal ruling (حكم شرعي) and every act of injustice or excess. However, the coupling of "the Apostle" with Him suggests that what is intended is something in which the Apostle has a specific role. Thus, it becomes almost certain that it refers to that which leads to the nullification of the effects of the authority (ولاية) granted to the Apostle by Allah, the Glorified—such as the disbelievers waging war against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or highwaymen disrupting the public security (الأمن العام) which he established on earth through his authority. The sentence being followed by His saying: "and strive to cause corruption on the earth" specifies the intended meaning as spreading corruption on earth by disrupting security and highway robbery, rather than absolute warfare against the Muslims. Furthermore, necessity dictates that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) did not treat the combatants among the disbelievers, after prevailing over them and gaining victory, with these specific punishments of execution, crucifixion, amputation, and exile.

Moreover, the exception in the following verse serves as a contextual clue (قرينة) that the intended meaning of "warfare" (المحاربة) is the aforementioned spreading of corruption; for it is apparent that the repentance mentioned is from the act of warfare, not from polytheism (الشرك) or its like.

Therefore, what is intended by "warfare" and "corruption," according to what is apparent, is the disruption of public security (الأمن العام). Public security is only disrupted by creating public fear and allowing it to take the place of security. Naturally and habitually, this only occurs through the use of weapons that inherently threaten life. For this reason, it has been mentioned in the transmitted Sunnah (السنة) that "corruption in the land" is interpreted as unsheathing the sword and the like, as will be discussed in the following section on traditions, Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَنْ يُقَتَّلُوا أَوْ يُصَلَّبُوا** — "...is that they shall be executed or crucified..." (5:33). **Execution (التقتيل), crucifixion (التصليب), and amputation (التقطيع)** are in the intensive form (تفعيل) of killing, crucifying, and cutting, which implies intensity or an increase in the base meaning. The word "or (أَوْ)" denotes an alternative (ترديد) as opposed to a combination (جمع). As for whether this implies a specific order (ترتيب) or a choice (تخيير) between the options, this is understood from external clues, whether situational or verbal; thus, the verse is not devoid of ambiguity in this regard.

The Sunnah clarifies this, and it will be mentioned that what is narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) is that the four legal limits are ordered according to the degrees of corruption—such as one who unsheathes a sword and kills a soul and takes property, or kills only, or takes property only, or unsheathes a sword only—as will be detailed in the following section on traditions, Allah willing.

As for His saying: **أَوْ تُقَطَّعَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَرْجُلُهُمْ مِنْ خِلافٍ** — "...or their hands and feet shall be cut off from opposite sides..." (5:33). What is meant by "from opposite sides" (من خلاف) is that the amputation should involve the hand and the foot from sides opposite to each other, such as the right hand and the left foot. This serves as the clue that the intended meaning of cutting "hands and feet" is cutting some of them, not all—meaning one hand and one foot, while observing the opposition of sides.

As for His saying: **أَوْ يُنْفَوْا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ** — "...or they shall be banished from the land..." (5:33). **Banishment (النفي)** is expulsion and removal, and it is interpreted in the Sunnah as being expelled from one city to another.

There are other jurisprudential (فقهية) discussions regarding this verse that should be sought in books of jurisprudence.

His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكَ لَهُمْ خِزْيٌ فِي الدُّنْيا وَلَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ** — "That shall be a disgrace for them in this world, and in the Hereafter there will be a great punishment for them" (5:33). **Disgrace (الخزي)** is public scandal (الفضيحة), and the meaning is clear.

This verse has been used as evidence that the execution of a legal limit (الحد) upon a criminal does not necessarily entail the removal of the punishment of the Hereafter, which is true in general.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَقْدِرُوا عَلَيْهِمْ** — "excepting those who repent before you capture them..." (5:34). However, after they are apprehended and the evidence is established, the legal limit is not dropped. As for His saying, the Exalted: **فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — "...then know that Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (5:34), it is an allusion (كناية) to the lifting of the legal limit from them. This verse is among the instances where forgiveness (المغفرة) relates to something other than matters of the Hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَابْتَغُوا إِلَيْهِ الْوَسِيلَةَ** — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and seek the means of recourse to Him..." (5:35). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "**The means of recourse (الوسيلة)** is reaching a thing through desire (رغبة), and it is more specific than 'connection' (الوصيلة) because it includes the meaning of desire. The Exalted said: 'and seek the means of recourse to Him.' The reality of the means of recourse to Allah, the Exalted, is observing His path through knowledge (العلم) and worship (العبادة), and seeking the noble qualities of the Sharia (الشريعة). It is like 'proximity' (القربة)." Since it is a type of reaching, and it is nothing but a spiritual reaching and connection that joins the servant to his Lord and links this one to that one—and there is no link that binds the servant to his Lord except the humility of servitude (ذلة العبودية)—then the "means of recourse" is the realization of the reality of servitude and directing the face of destitution and poverty toward His Presence, the Exalted. This is the connecting means. As for knowledge and action, they are merely among its requirements and instruments, as is apparent, unless "knowledge and action" are applied to this state itself.

From here it becomes clear that what is intended by His saying: **وَجاهِدُوا فِي سَبِيلِهِ** — "...and strive in His way..." (5:35) is absolute striving (الجهاد), which encompasses both the struggle against the self (جهاد النفس) and the struggle against the disbelievers (جهاد الكفار) together; for there is no evidence to restrict it to the struggle against disbelievers, especially given the connection of the sentence to what preceded it regarding the seeking of the means of recourse, the meaning of which you have recognized. Furthermore, the two following verses, with the reasoning they contain, are more consistent with the intention of absolute striving in His saying: "and strive in His way."

Nevertheless, it is possible that what is intended by "striving" (الجهاد) is fighting (القتال) against the disbelievers, considering that the qualification of striving as being "in the way of Allah" (في سبيل الله) only occurs in verses commanding striving in the sense of fighting. As for the more general sense, it is devoid of this qualification, such as His saying, the Exalted: "As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them to Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous" (29:69). Based on this, the command to strive in His way after the command to seek the means of recourse to Him is of the nature of mentioning the specific after the general to emphasize its importance. Perhaps the command to seek the means of recourse to Him after the command to be God-conscious (التقوى) is also of this nature.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ أَنَّ لَهُمْ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "Indeed as for the faithless, should they possess all that is on the earth..." (5:36) until the end of the two verses. Its apparent meaning—as previously indicated—is that it serves as a justification (تعليل) for the content of the preceding verse. The summary is: it is incumbent upon you to be God-conscious, seek the means of recourse to Him, and strive in His way, for this is a matter of concern to you in averting a painful, permanent punishment from yourselves. There is no substitute that can take its place; for if those who disbelieved—and thus did not fear Allah, nor seek the means of recourse to Him, nor strive in His way—possessed all that is on the earth (which is the maximum of what a human usually desires of worldly possession) and then as much again were added to it so they had double what is on the earth, and they then wished to ransom themselves with it from the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, it would not be accepted from them. For them is a painful punishment; they will want to emerge from the Fire—which is the punishment—but they shall not emerge from it, because it is a lasting, permanent punishment upon them that shall never leave them.

In the verse, there is an indication, firstly, that punishment is the primary state close to man, and it is only averted by faith (الإيمان) and godwariness (التقوى), as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "There is none of you but will come to it: a [matter] finally decided by your Lord. Then We will deliver those who were God-conscious, and leave the wrongdoers in it, fallen on their knees" (19:71-72); and likewise His saying: "Indeed man is in loss, except those who have faith and do righteous deeds..." (103:2-3).

Secondly: that the original human primordial nature (الفطرة)—which is that which feels pain from the Fire—is not nullified in them nor absent from them; otherwise, they would not feel pain nor be punished by it, nor would they desire to emerge from it.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالسَّارِقُ وَالسَّارِقَةُ فَاقْطَعُوا أَيْدِيَهُما** — "As for the thief, man or woman, cut off their hands..." (5:38). The "and (الواو)" is for commencement (استيناف), and the speech is in the position of detailing (تفصيل); thus it is in the sense of: "As for the male thief and the female thief..." etc. For this reason, the "fa (الفاء)" entered the predicate, namely His saying: "cut off their hands," because it is in the sense of the response to "as for" (أما), as has been said.

As for the use of the plural in His saying: **أَيْدِيَهُما** — "their hands" (literally 'hands of the two of them') while the dual is intended, it has been said that this is a common usage. The reason for it is that some or most organs in a human are paired, such as the two horns, the two eyes, the two ears, the two hands, the two legs, and the two feet. When these are annexed to a dual, they become four, and they take the plural form, such as "their eyes," "their hands," "their legs," and the like. Then the plural became standard in speech whenever an organ is annexed to a dual, even if the organ is not among the paired ones, as in their saying: "I filled their backs and their bellies with striking." The Exalted said: "If you two repent to Allah, for your hearts have certainly swerved" (66:4). **The hand (اليد)** is what is below the shoulder, and what is intended in the verse is the right hand according to the interpretation of the Sunnah. The "cutting of the hand" is realized by separating some of its parts or all of them from the body with a cutting instrument.

His saying: **جَزاءُ بِما كَسَبا نَكالاً مِنَ اللهِ** — "...as a retribution for what they have earned, as an exemplary punishment from Allah" (5:38). It is apparent that it is in the position of a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the "cutting" understood from His saying: "so cut off," meaning: while the cutting is a retribution for what they earned and an exemplary punishment from Allah. **Exemplary punishment (النكال)** is the penalty with which a criminal is punished so that he may cease his criminality and others among the people may take heed.

This meaning—namely, that the cutting is an exemplary punishment—is what justifies the branching of His saying: **فَمَنْ تابَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ظُلْمِهِ وَأَصْلَحَ فَإِنَّ اللهَ يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِ** — "But whoever repents after his wrongdoing and reforms, then Allah shall accept his repentance..." (5:39). That is, since the cutting is an exemplary punishment intended to make the punished one return from his disobedience, then whoever repents after his wrongdoing with a sincere repentance and then reforms and does not hover around theft—and this is a matter by which the meaning of repentance is confirmed—then Allah will turn toward him and return to him with forgiveness and mercy, for Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. The Exalted said: "What would Allah have to do with punishing you if you are grateful and faithful? And Allah is appreciative, all-knowing" (4:147).

There are many other jurisprudential discussions in the verse for the student to consult in books of jurisprudence.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — "Do you not know that to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth?..." (5:40). This is in the position of a justification (تعليل) for what was mentioned in the previous verse regarding the acceptance of the repentance of the male and female thief if they repent and reform after their wrongdoing. For since Allah, the Glorified, possesses the kingdom (الملك) of the heavens and the earth, and the king has the right to judge in his kingdom and among his subjects as he likes and desires of punishment or mercy, it was for Him, the Exalted, to punish whom He wills and forgive whom He wills according to wisdom (الحكمة) and benefit (المصلحة); thus He punishes the male and female thief if they do not repent, and forgives them if they do.

His saying: **وَاللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** — "...And Allah has power over all things" (5:40) is in the position of a justification for His saying: "to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth." For kingdom (الملك - with *damma*) is among the aspects of power (القدرة), just as ownership (الملك - with *kasra*) is among the branches of creation and origination—meaning the Divine self-subsistence (القيمومة الإلهية).

The explanation of this is: Allah, the Exalted, is the Creator of things and their Originator; thus there is nothing except that what it possesses of itself and the effects of itself belong to Allah, the Glorified. He is the Giver of what He gave and the Withholder of what He withheld. Thus, He has the right to dispose of everything, and this is ownership (الملك - with *kasra*). The Exalted said: "Say, 'Allah is the creator of all things, and He is the One, the All-paramount'" (13:16); and He said: "Allah—there is no god except Him—is the Living One, the All-sustainer. Neither slumber nor sleep overtakes Him. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth..." (2:255). Furthermore, He, the Exalted, is powerful over any disposal He likes and desires, since whatever thing is assumed, it is from Him; thus for Him is the execution of judgment and the effectiveness of will, and this is kingdom (الملك - with *damma*) and sovereignty (السلطنة) over everything. Thus He, the Exalted, is the Owner (مالك) because He is the Sustainer (قيوم) over everything, and the King (ملك) because He is powerful, not incapable, nor prevented from the execution of His wish and will.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Abu Salih, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "A group of people from the tribe of Dabbah came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while they were ill. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to them: 'Stay with me, and when you recover, I will send you in a military expedition.' They said: 'Take us out of Medina.' So he sent them to the camels of charity (إبل الصدقة) to drink from their urine and eat from their milk. When they recovered and grew strong, they killed three of those who were with the camels. News reached the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), so he sent ‘Ali (peace be upon him) to them. He found them in a valley, confused, unable to find a way out, near the land of Yemen. He captured them and brought them to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and this verse was revealed: **إِنَّما جَزاءُ الَّذِينَ يُحارِبُونَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَساداً أَنْ يُقَتَّلُوا أَوْ يُصَلَّبُوا أَوْ تُقَطَّعَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَرْجُلُهُمْ مِنْ خِلافٍ** — 'Indeed the requital of those who wage war against Allah and His Apostle, and strive to cause corruption on the earth, is that they shall be executed or crucified, or their hands and feet shall be cut off from opposite sides...'"

I say: It was also narrated in *al-Tahdhib* with its chain from Abu Salih from him (peace be upon him) with a slight variation. Al-‘Ayyashi narrated it in his *Tafsir* from him (peace be upon him) and added at the end: "So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) chose to cut their hands and feet from opposite sides." The story is narrated in the collections of the Ahl al-Sunnah, including the *Six Sahihs*, through various paths with differences in details. Among them is what occurred in some that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), after capturing them, cut their hands and feet from opposite sides and branded their eyes (سمل أعينهم). In some: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) killed some of them, crucified, amputated, and branded the eyes." In some: "He branded their eyes because they had branded the eyes of the shepherds." In some: "Allah forbade him from branding the eyes, and the verse was revealed as a reproach to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding this mutilation (المثلة)." In some: "He intended to brand their eyes but did not," among other reports.

The narrations transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) are devoid of any mention of branding the eyes.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from ‘Amr ibn ‘Uthman ibn ‘Ubaydillah al-Mada’ini from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him), who said: "He was asked about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **إِنَّما جَزاءُ الَّذِينَ يُحارِبُونَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَساداً أَنْ يُقَتَّلُوا** (the verse)—what is it that, if he does it, he deserves one of these four?"

He said: "**إذا حارب الله ورسوله ـ وسعى في الأرض فسادا فقتل قتل به ، وإن قتل وأخذ المال قتل وصلب ، وإن أخذ المال ولم يقتل ـ قطعت يده ورجله من خلاف ، وإن شهر السيف فحارب الله ورسوله ـ وسعى في الأرض فسادا ولم يقتل ـ ولم يأخذ المال نفي من الأرض** — 'If he wages war against Allah and His Apostle and strives to cause corruption on the earth and kills, he is killed for it. If he kills and takes property, he is killed and crucified. If he takes property and does not kill, his hand and foot are cut off from opposite sides. And if he unsheathes the sword and wages war against Allah and His Apostle and strives to cause corruption on the earth but does not kill and does not take property, he is banished from the land.'"

I said: "How is he banished from the land, and what is the limit of his banishment?" He said: "**ينفى من المصر الذي فعل فيه ما فعل إلى مصر غيره ، ويكتب إلى أهل ذلك المصر أنه منفي ـ فلا تجالسوه ولا تبايعوه ولا تناكحوه ولا تؤاكلوه ولا تشاربوه فيفعل ذلك به سنة ـ فإن خرج من ذلك المصر إلى غيره ـ كتب إليهم بمثل ذلك حتى تتم السنة** — 'He is banished from the city in which he did what he did to another city, and it is written to the people of that city that he is an exile—so do not sit with him, nor trade with him, nor marry him, nor eat with him, nor drink with him. This is done to him for a year. If he leaves that city for another, the same is written to them until the year is complete.'" I said: "What if he heads toward the land of polytheism (أرض الشرك) to enter it?" He said: "**إن توجه إلى أرض الشرك ليدخلها قوتل أهلها** — 'If he heads toward the land of polytheism to enter it, its people are fought [to prevent him].'"

I say: It was narrated by al-Shaykh in *al-Tahdhib* and al-‘Ayyashi in his *Tafsir* from Abu Ishaq al-Mada’ini from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The narrations regarding these meanings are widely transmitted (مستفيضة) from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). Likewise, this has been narrated through several paths from the Ahl al-Sunnah. In some of their narrations, the Imam has the choice: if he wishes he kills, if he wishes he crucifies, if he wishes he cuts the hands and feet from opposite sides, and if he wishes he banishes. Similar to this is what occurred in some narrations of the elite (الخاصة) regarding the Imam having the choice, such as what was narrated in *al-Kafi* with a chain from Jamil ibn Darraj from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse. He said: "I said: 'What is upon them of these legal limits that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, named?' He said: '**ذلك إلى الإمام إن شاء قطع ، وإن شاء نفى ، وإن شاء صلب ، وإن شاء قتل** — "That is up to the Imam: if he wishes he amputates, if he wishes he banishes, if he wishes he crucifies, and if he wishes he kills."' I said: 'Banishment to where?' He (peace be upon him) said: 'He is banished from one city to another.' And he said: 'Indeed ‘Ali (peace be upon him) banished two men from Kufa to Basra.'"

The complete discussion is in jurisprudence, except that the verse is not devoid of an allusion to the order (الترتيب) between the legal limits according to the variation in the levels of corruption. For the alternative (الترديد) between execution, crucifixion, amputation, and banishment—which are matters that are not equal or balanced, but rather differ in terms of intensity and weakness—is a rational clue (قرينة عقلية) for that.

Just as the apparent meaning of the verse is that these are legal limits for warfare and corruption; so whoever unsheathes a sword and strives to cause corruption on the earth or kills a soul, he is only killed because he is a combatant spreading corruption (محارب مفسد), and this is not a retaliation (قصاص) taken from him for killing a protected soul. Thus, the execution is not dropped even if the guardians of the slain person agree to the blood money (الدية).

As narrated by al-‘Ayyashi in his *Tafsir* from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), and in it: "Abu ‘Ubaydah said: 'May Allah rectify you—what if the guardians of the slain person pardon him?' Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: '**إن عفوا عنه فعلى الإمام أن يقتله ـ لأنه قد حارب وقتل وسرق** — "If they pardon him, it is upon the Imam to kill him, because he has waged war, killed, and stolen."' Abu ‘Ubaydah said: 'If the guardians of the slain person want to take the blood money from him and leave him, do they have that right?' He said: '**لا ، عليه القتل** — "No, upon him is execution."'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Abi Shaybah, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Abi al-Dunya in the book *al-Ashraf*, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Sha‘bi, who said: "Harithah ibn Badr al-Tamimi was from the people of Basra; he had spread corruption in the land and waged war. He spoke to men from Quraysh to seek amnesty for him from ‘Ali, but they refused. So he went to Sa‘id ibn Qays al-Hamdani, who then went to ‘Ali and said: 'O Commander of the Faithful, what is the requital for those who wage war against Allah and His Apostle and strive to cause corruption on the earth?' He said: 'That they shall be executed or crucified, or their hands and feet shall be cut off from opposite sides, or they shall be banished from the land.' Then he said: 'Excepting those who repent before you capture them.'

Sa‘id said: 'Even if it were Harithah ibn Badr?' He said: 'Even if it were Harithah ibn Badr.' Sa‘id said: 'This is Harithah ibn Badr who has come repentant, so is he safe?' He said: 'Yes.' So he brought him to him, and he pledged allegiance to him, and he accepted that from him and wrote for him an amnesty."

I say: Sa‘id's saying in the narration, "Even if it were Harithah ibn Badr," is an addition he joined to the verse to demonstrate its general applicability to every repentant person after warfare and corruption, and this is frequent in speech.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from Sawrah ibn Kulayb, who said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'A man leaves his house intending the mosque or intending some need, and a man meets him, follows him, strikes him, and takes his garment?' He said: 'What do those before you say about it?' I said: 'They say: this is public thuggery (ذعارة معلنة), and the combatant (المحارب) is only in the villages of polytheism.' He said: '**أيها أعظم حرمة : دار الإسلام أو دار الشرك؟** — "Which is greater in sanctity: the Abode of Islam (دار الإسلام) or the Abode of Polytheism (دار الشرك)?"' I said: 'The Abode of Islam.' He said: '**هؤلاء من أهل هذه الآية : إِنَّما جَزاءُ الَّذِينَ يُحارِبُونَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ** — "These are among the people of this verse: 'Indeed the requital of those who wage war against Allah and His Apostle...'"'"

I say: What the narrator pointed to regarding the saying of the people is what occurred in some narrations of the general public, as in some narrations of the occasion of revelation (سبب النزول) from al-Dahhak, who said: "This verse was revealed concerning the polytheists." And what is in the *Tafsir* of al-Tabari: that ‘Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan wrote to Anas asking him about this verse, so Anas wrote to him informing him: "This verse was revealed concerning those individuals from the ‘Urayniyyin, and they were from Bajilah." Anas said: "They apostatized from Islam, killed the shepherd, drove away the camels, made the path fearful, and committed forbidden sexual acts. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) asked Gabriel about the judgment regarding one who wages war, and he said: 'Whoever steals, makes the path fearful, and deems forbidden sexual acts lawful, then crucify him,'" among other narrations.

The verse, by its general wording, supports what is in the report of *al-Kafi*. It is well known that the occasion of revelation does not necessitate the restriction of the apparent meaning of the verse.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَابْتَغُوا إِلَيْهِ الْوَسِيلَةَ** (the verse), he said: "He said: '**تقربوا إليه بالإمام** — "Seek proximity to Him through the Imam."'"

I say: Meaning through his obedience; so it is of the nature of application (الجري) and fitting the concept to an instance (المصداق). Similar to it is what is narrated from Ibn Shahr Ashub, who said: "The Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) said regarding His saying, the Exalted: 'and seek the means of recourse to Him': '**أنا وسيلته** — "I am His means of recourse."'"

Close to it is what is in *Basa’ir al-Darajat*, with its chain from Salman from ‘Ali (peace be upon him). It is possible that the two narrations are of the nature of esoteric interpretation (التأويل), so reflect upon them.

In *al-Majma‘*: It is narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "**سلوا الله لي الوسيلة فإنها درجة في الجنة ـ لا ينالها إلا عبد واحد وأرجو أن أكون أنا هو** — 'Ask Allah for the *Wasilah* for me, for it is a degree in Paradise that only one servant shall attain, and I hope that I am he.'"

In *al-Ma‘ani*, with its chain from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'When you ask Allah, ask for the *Wasilah* for me.' So we asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about the *Wasilah*, and he said: '**هي درجتي في الجنة** — "It is my degree in Paradise..."'" (the tradition), and it is a long, well-known tradition called the Tradition of the *Wasilah*.

If you reflect upon the tradition and the application of the verse's meaning to it, you will find that the "means of recourse" (الوسيلة) is the station of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with his Lord through which he seeks proximity to Him, the Exalted, and to which his pure progeny (آله الطاهرون) and then the righteous of his nation are joined. It has been mentioned in some narrations from them (peace be upon them): "The Messenger of Allah is holding onto the waist-tie (حجزة) of his Lord, and we are holding onto his waist-tie, and you are holding onto our waist-tie."

To this returns what we mentioned regarding the two narrations of al-Qummi and Ibn Shahr Ashub, that it is possible they are of the nature of esoteric interpretation. Perhaps we will be granted the success to explain this meaning in a suitable place in what is to come.

Among what is joined to these narrations is what al-‘Ayyashi narrated from Abu Basir, who said: "I heard Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) saying: 'The enemies of ‘Ali are those who will abide forever in the Fire.' Allah said: **وَما هُمْ بِخارِجِينَ مِنْها** — '...and they shall not emerge from it' (5:37)."

In *al-Burhan*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَالسَّارِقُ وَالسَّارِقَةُ فَاقْطَعُوا أَيْدِيَهُما** (the verse): from *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain from Abu Ibrahim (peace be upon him), who said: "**تقطع يد السارق ويترك إبهامه وراحته ، وتقطع رجله ويترك عقبه يمشي عليها** — 'The hand of the thief is cut off and his thumb and palm are left; and his foot is cut off and his heel is left for him to walk upon.'"

In *al-Tahdhib* also, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'For how much is the hand of a thief cut?' He said: '**في ربع دينار** — "For a quarter of a dinar."' I said to him: 'For two dirhams?' He said: '**في ربع دينار بلغ الدينار ما بلغ** — "For a quarter of a dinar, whatever the value of the dinar may reach."' I said to him: 'What do you say of one who steals less than a quarter of a dinar—does the name "thief" apply to him when he steals? And is he a thief in the sight of Allah in that state?' He said: '**كل من سرق من مسلم شيئا ـ قد حواه وأحرزه فهو يقع عليه اسم السارق ، وهو عند الله سارق ـ ولكن لا تقطع إلا في ربع دينار أو أكثر ، ولو قطعت يد السارق فيما هو أقل من ربع دينار ـ لألفيت عامة الناس مقطعين** — "Everyone who steals something from a Muslim that he had collected and secured, the name 'thief' applies to him, and he is a thief in the sight of Allah. However, the hand is not cut except for a quarter of a dinar or more. If the hand of the thief were cut for what is less than a quarter of a dinar, you would find most people amputated."'"

I say: He (peace be upon him) means by his saying, "If the hand of the thief were cut..." that in the ruling of amputation there is a mitigation from Allah as a mercy from Him to His servants. This meaning—namely, the restriction of the ruling to the theft of a quarter of a dinar or more—is narrated through some paths of the general public as well. In the two *Sahihs* of al-Bukhari and Muslim, with their chains from ‘Aishah, that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "The hand of a thief is not cut except for a quarter of a dinar and upwards."

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyashi, from Sama‘ah from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): that he said: "**إذا أخذ السارق فقطع وسط الكف ـ فإن عاد قطعت رجله من وسط القدم ـ فإن عاد استودع السجن ـ فإن سرق في السجن قتل** — 'If the thief is taken, the middle of the palm is cut. If he returns, his foot is cut from the middle of the foot. If he returns, he is committed to prison. If he steals in prison, he is killed.'"

In it, from Zurarah from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): regarding a man who stole and his right hand was cut, then he stole and his left foot was cut, then he stole a third time? He said: "**كان أمير المؤمنين عليه‌السلام يخلده في السجن ـ ويقول : إني لأستحيي من ربي ـ أن أدعه بلا يد يستنظف بها ـ ولا رجل يمشي بها إلى حاجته** — 'The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) used to imprison him for life and say: "Indeed, I feel shame before my Lord to leave him without a hand with which he cleanses himself, or a foot with which he walks to his need."'"

He said: "When he cut the hand, he cut it below the joint; and when he cut the foot, he cut it below the ankles." He said: "And he did not believe in neglecting any of the legal limits."

In it: from Zurqan, the companion of Ibn Abi Du’ad and his close friend, who said: "Ibn Abi Du’ad returned one day from al-Mu‘tasim while he was grieved. I spoke to him about that, and he said: 'I wished today that I had died twenty years ago.' I said to him: 'And why is that?' He said: 'Because of what happened from this black man—Abu Ja‘far Muhammad ibn ‘Ali ibn Musa—today before the Commander of the Faithful al-Mu‘tasim.' I said: 'And how was that?' He said: 'A thief confessed against himself to theft and asked the Caliph to purify him by establishing the legal limit upon him. So he gathered the jurists (الفقهاء) for that in his assembly, and Muhammad ibn ‘Ali was brought. He asked us about the amputation—at which place must it be cut? I said: "From the wrist (الكرسوع)," because of the saying of Allah regarding *tayammum*: "so wipe your faces and your hands," and a group agreed with me on that.

Others said: "Rather, the amputation must be from the elbow." He said: "And what is the evidence for that?" They said: "Because when Allah said: 'and your hands up to the elbows' regarding the ritual washing (الغسل), it indicated that the limit of the hand is the elbow."

He said: "Then he turned to Muhammad ibn ‘Ali and said: 'What do you say about this, O Abu Ja‘far?' He said: 'The people have already spoken about it, O Commander of the Faithful.' He said: 'Leave what they have spoken; what do you have?' He said: 'Excuse me from this, O Commander of the Faithful.' He said: 'I adjure you by Allah to inform me of what you have regarding it.' He said: 'Since you have adjured me by Allah, I say: they have erred regarding the Sunnah. For the amputation must be from the joint of the base of the fingers, and the palm is left.' He said: 'And what is the proof for that?' He said: 'The saying of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Prostration is upon seven organs: the face, the two hands, the two knees, and the two feet." If his hand is cut from the wrist or the elbow, he will have no hand left to prostrate upon. And Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, said: "And that the places of prostration (المساجد) belong to Allah"—meaning these seven organs upon which one prostrates—"so do not invoke anyone along with Allah." And what belongs to Allah is not cut.'

He said: 'Al-Mu‘tasim was amazed by that, and he ordered the amputation of the thief's hand from the joint of the fingers, excluding the palm.' Ibn Abi Du’ad said: 'My resurrection was established [out of distress], and I wished I were not alive.'"

Ibn Abi Zurqan said: "Ibn Abi Du’ad said: 'I went to al-Mu‘tasim after three days and said: "Indeed, sincerity toward the Commander of the Faithful is obligatory, and I am speaking to him with that which I know will enter me into the Fire." He said: "And what is it?" I said: "When the Commander of the Faithful gathers in his assembly the jurists of his subjects and their scholars for an occurring matter of the matters of religion, and he asks them for the ruling regarding it, and they inform him of what they have of the ruling in that—while his sons, his commanders, his ministers, and his scribes are present in the assembly, and the people have heard of that from behind his door—then he leaves all their sayings for the saying of a man to whose Imamate half of this nation subscribes, and they claim that he is more worthy of his station than him—then he judges by his judgment instead of the judgment of the jurists?" He said: 'His color changed, and he became alert to what I alerted him to, and he said: "May Allah reward you well for your sincerity."

He said: 'On the fourth day, he ordered so-and-so among the scribes of his ministers to invite him to his house. He invited him, but he refused to answer him, saying: "You know that I do not attend your assemblies." He said: "I am only inviting you for food, and I would love for you to tread upon my carpets and enter my house so I may be blessed by that. And so-and-so among the ministers of the Caliph would love to meet you." So he went to him. When he ate from it, he felt the pain of the poison. He called for his mount, and the master of the house asked him to stay. He said: "My leaving your house is better for you." He remained in that state for that day and night until he passed away.'"

I say: The story has been narrated through other paths. We only cited the narration in its length, like some of the preceding repeated narrations, because it contains precise Quranic investigations that assist in understanding the verses.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ahmad, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Umar: "A woman stole during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and her right hand was cut. She said: 'Is there any repentance for me, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'Yes, today you are as free from your sin as the day your mother gave birth to you.' Then Allah revealed in Surah al-Ma’idah: **فَمَنْ تابَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ظُلْمِهِ وَأَصْلَحَ فَإِنَّ اللهَ يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — 'But whoever repents after his wrongdoing and reforms, then Allah shall accept his repentance. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful' (5:39)."

I say: The narration is of the nature of application (تطبيق), and the connection of the verse to what preceded it and their simultaneous revelation is apparent.
